coredata.dc:title: Aerosol derived carbon dots decorated boron nitride supported Zn-doped MoS2 for high performing flexible asymmetric supercapacitor
coredata.dc:creator: Maity, Chandan Kumar
De, Shrabani
Panigrahi, Asutosh
Acharya, Sourav
Verma, Kartikey
Kim, Myung Jong
Nayak, Ganesh Chandra
coredata.dc:description: Alternative finding of toxic pollutant materials in proper utilization is a great challenge. Toxic and pollutant cigarette smoke having different π-conjugated organic components may work well as a precursor for carbon dots (CDs). In this work, cigarette smoke aerosol has been used for the synthesis of CDs and CDs have been further used for the fabrication of supercapacitor electrode. This methodology could be able to mitigate the two major problems-energy crisis and environment pollution. The smart combination of EDLC-type CDs deposited boron nitride (BN) and pseudocapacitive type Zn-doped MoS2 was able to enhance the supercapacitive performances. Heteroatom doping into MoS2 enhances the capacitive performances through the effective Faradic reactions. CDs and CDs deposited BN (CDs@BN) have been characterized using different characterization techniques. Zn-doped MoS2 has been uniformly grown on the CDs decorated BN (Zn–MoS2/CDs@BN) resulting in the stabilization of the electrode system. Zn–MoS2/CDs@BN nanohybrid delivered a specific capacitance (Sp.C) of 397 F/g in three-electrode system. Further, the asymmetric supercapacitor (ASC) device using Zn–MoS2/CDs@BN as cathode demonstrated wide potential range (up to 1.8 V) with a volumetric energy density of 3.42 mW h/cm3 and retaining a capacitance of ∼89% after 10,000 charging-discharging processes. Moreover, a flexible ASC device was made-up using viscous ionic electrolyte and it was able to glow a red LED, while it was bended in different angles. The methods described here will give a straightforward approach for using toxic-pollutant cigarette smoke to fabricate supercapacitor electrodes in order to compensate the upcoming energy crisis in a sustainable way.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Alternative finding of toxic pollutant materials in proper utilization is a great challenge. Toxic and pollutant cigarette smoke having different π-conjugated organic components may work well as a precursor for carbon dots (CDs). In this work, cigarette smoke aerosol has been used for the synthesis of CDs and CDs have been further used for the fabrication of supercapacitor electrode. This methodology could be able to mitigate the two major problems-energy crisis and environment pollution. The smart combination of EDLC-type CDs deposited boron nitride (BN) and pseudocapacitive type Zn-doped MoS2 was able to enhance the supercapacitive performances. Heteroatom doping into MoS2 enhances the capacitive performances through the effective Faradic reactions. CDs and CDs deposited BN (CDs@BN) have been characterized using different characterization techniques. Zn-doped MoS2 has been uniformly grown on the CDs decorated BN (Zn–MoS2/CDs@BN) resulting in the stabilization of the electrode system. Zn–MoS2/CDs@BN nanohybrid delivered a specific capacitance (Sp.C) of 397 F/g in three-electrode system. Further, the asymmetric supercapacitor (ASC) device using Zn–MoS2/CDs@BN as cathode demonstrated wide potential range (up to 1.8 V) with a volumetric energy density of 3.42 mW h/cm3 and retaining a capacitance of ∼89% after 10,000 charging-discharging processes. Moreover, a flexible ASC device was made-up using viscous ionic electrolyte and it was able to glow a red LED, while it was bended in different angles. The methods described here will give a straightforward approach for using toxic-pollutant cigarette smoke to fabricate supercapacitor electrodes in order to compensate the upcoming energy crisis in a sustainable way.

Cigarette smoke aerosol derived carbon dots decorated boron nitride supported Zn-doped MoS2 for high performance flexible asymmetric supercapacitor device in low-cost viable ionic electrolyte.
Image 1

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: With rapid advancements of global civilization, the energy demand has already reached 606 × 1018 J as of 2015, and it is further expected to increase up to 776 × 1018 J by 2040 [1]. The high demand of energy would lead to rapid fossil fuel consumption. Still, the required energy demand may not be fulfilled through the usage of fossil fuels. These all have prompted the significant research attention for the development of renewable energy storage devices. An electrochemical energy storage device, supercapacitor, can store charges electrostatically across the electric double layer by polarizing the electrolytic solution [2]. Supercapacitor retains higher stability and power density but lower energy density as compared to battery [3]. To decouple the energy and power characteristics of a battery-based energy storage system, a supercapacitor can be introduced, consequently improving the sizing while satisfying the power and energy requirements, and undoubtedly enhancing its lifetime [3]. However, lower energy density is the major problem associated with the existing supercapacitor electrode materials, which needs to be addressed by developing innovative electrode materials for supercapacitors through the smart permutation of electric double layer capacitor and pseudocapacitor.

BN, a graphene analogous sp2 hybridized polar inorganic compound, attracts substantial research interest for the development of energy storage devices [4–6]. In BN, B–N bond is partially ionic in nature, which induces the polarity within BN [7]. In the B–N bond, the electrons are polarized towards N, due to which N holds a partially negative charge and B possesses a partially positive charge [7]. Under external potential, BN can undergo charge separation due to its polar nature, which can be explored for charge storage. Most often, the electrolyte contact causes the B–N bond's oxidation state to shift, which can increase the electrodes' pseudocapacitive behavior [8]. However, the major issue with BN is its high band gap, which can be tuned by hybridization, substitution, doping, and functionalization [7,9,10]. The formation of heterostructure as a result of BN and carbonaceous material hybridization could increase the electrochemical activity and mobility of charge carriers [5]. Kuila et al. have studied the tuning of BN's band gap in presence of carbon materials [8]. This work targets the hybridization of BN with CDs, synthesized from toxic pollutant cigarette smoke, through the deposition of CDs on BN surface to improve the electrochemical properties, surface charge storage mechanism as well as to tune the electronic band structure.

CDs are being widely used in different electronic fields due to their uniform monodispersed shape having 0–10 nm size, high surface area, highly tunable surface chemistry as well as their desirable optical and electronic properties [11]. Additionally, CDs are widely being considered as supportive electrode materials that enhance the interacting area between electrolyte and electrode, ionic motion and electron transport in supercapacitor [11–14]. Herein, cigarette smoke aerosol has been used to synthesize CDs, one of the major components of the synthesized electrode materials for asymmetric supercapacitor. Cigarette smoke, containing nearly 5000 estimated organic and inorganic chemicals, is a complex, dynamic, and reactive mixture [15–17]. Water extract of cigarette smoke aerosol, which contains different carbon compounds, can produce the carbon dots (CDs) during hydrothermal process [18]. In this work, environment pollutant and toxic cigarette smoke containing different π-conjugated carbon compounds have been used for the synthesis of CDs. The methodology demonstrates the utilization of cigarette smoke to mitigate the environmental pollution and health issues; and to develop an energy storage device with the impending energy crisis through a sustainable and green way.

Appropriate materialization of pseudocapacitive and EDLC materials can enhance the electrochemical performances of supercapacitor including its energy density. In case of composite hybrid electrodes, the disadvantages of individual materials can be minimized through their smart combinations. He et al. showed that through the smart combination of carbon-coated Ni–Mn layered double hydroxide with Ni3S2, the electrochemical performances of the supercapacitor were increased and the resultant composite electrode exhibited the Sp.C of 3490 mF/cm2 at 2 mA/cm2 and holds 84.25% capacitance after 5000 cycles [19]. Sometimes to improve the supercapacitive electrochemical performances of metal organic frameworks, they are often combined with metal hydroxides [20–22]. Both BN and carbon materials, mainly CDs, shows the EDLC characteristics having lower energy density. Therefore, CDs deposited BN has been further coupled with the pseudocapacitive material Zn-doped MoS2. MoS2, a promising 2D material, possesses layered structure having the band gap of 1.23 eV [23,24]. Its hexagonal like structure with 0.32 nm spacing between the layers and large surface area make MoS2 as an appealing component for the synthesis of electrode materials [24,25]. Such a layered structure of MoS2 facilitates electrochemical performances through the intercalation and deintercalation of electrolyte ions [26]. MoS2 provides higher theoretical capacitance than graphite as well as it possesses superior ionic conductivity as compared to other metal oxides [26]. Both charge transfer and surface limited electrostatic reactions are mainly responsible for the charge storage mechanism of MoS2 [27–29]. Hetero atom doping within the transition metal sulfide can produce defects and tunable surface properties, which can further tune the electronic structure of the lattice resulting in desired electrochemical performances [30,31]. Moreover, doping can inhibit the hole (h+)/(e−) recombination [32,33]. Zn may be the suitable metal that can be doped within the MoS2 lattice. Through the doping of Zn within MoS2 lattice, possibility of redox reaction will increase due to the presence of two metal centers, which can further enhance the energy density of the electrode. When compared to monometal components, bimetallic centers show superior electronic conductivity and more abundant active sites [34]. Along with the electrode materials, the role of electrolytes is very important for any electrochemical device [35]. Therefore, suitable combination of electrode and electrolyte can enhance the electrochemical activity of the supercapacitor including energy density and stability.

Herein, we have reported Zn-doped MoS2 @CDs deposited BN nanohybrid for enhanced electrochemical performances. Initially, cigarette smoke aerosol derived CDs were deposited on BN nanosheets. To improve the electrochemical efficiencies of CDs@BN, Zn-doped MoS2 was materialized with CDs@BN. Heteroatom doping within MoS2 lattice increased the effective redox reactions due to the presence of two metal centers. This work targets the utilization of toxic pollutant cigarette smoke to produce a value-added advanced supercapacitor electrode with Zn-doped MoS2 and BN having enhanced energy density.

Initially, cigarette smoke having different organic components has been extracted in water and it was treated at 200 °C for 24 h to obtained CDs. Fig. S1 shows the glass apparatus used to prepare the water extract of smoke aerosol obtained from a burning cigarette and the brown solution of the cigarette smoke extract. Using water extract of cigarette smoke as CDs precursor, CDs deposited BN was synthesized through simple hydrothermal technique. In this aspect, 50 mg of BN was combined with different amounts of cigarette extract—25 ml and 15 ml—to synthesize CDs@BN and CDs@BN-1, respectively. Both CDs@BN and CDs@BN-1 were initially optimized with respect to dispersion of CDs on BN, surface charge, optical properties, band gap as well as electrochemical properties. Consequently, the optimized CDs deposited BN was used to synthesize the composite electrode with MoS2 and Zn-Doped MoS2 for the fabrication of ASC device. Synthesis methods of CDs from cigarette smoke, CDs deposited BN, and CDs deposited BN based nanohybrid are graphically represented in Scheme 1 . This methodology demonstrates an alternative way to use toxic pollutant cigarette smoke for the fabrication of energy storage device, a toxic pollutant to value added product.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of carbon dots and carbon dots deposited BN electrodes
ce:section.ce:para: Surface morphology of CDs deposited BN (CDs@BN and CDs@BN-1) was investigated using FESEM analysis. Fig. S2 (a, b) displays the FESEM images of CDs@BN-1. During hydrothermal treatment of cigarette extract in presence of BN, CDs having very smaller size are deposited on the surface of BN. Cigarette extract contains different hazardous carbon containing components, which are responsible to from dots at elevated temperature, and they are deposited mainly on the BN surface. Deposition of CDs is clearly visualized in high magnification image of CDs@BN-1 (Fig. S2 (b)) and it can be further confirmed from the TEM analysis. FESEM images show that the deposition of CDs is mainly on the surface of BN and there is no intercalation of CDs between the BN layers. The FESEM images of CDs@BN are depicted in Fig. S2 (c, d). Same phenomena are also observed in case of CDs@BN. However, more disorderness of the BN sheets are observed, when dispersed solution of BN is treated with the 25 ml of cigarette extract. The degree of formation of CDs is more feasible in case of CDs@BN.

To investigate the layer orderliness of CDs@BN and CDs@BN-1, XRD analysis was performed, and corresponding XRD plot is represented in Fig. S3. Bare BN shows a distinctive intense sharp peak at 2θ = 26.8 (d = 0.33 nm) and comparatively lower intense peak at 2θ = 42.30 (d = 0.21 nm), equivalent to the planes (002) and (100), correspondingly (JCPDS No. 45–0893) [36]. After the deposition of CDs on BN surface, there are no change of the peak positions of BN for CDs@BN-1 and CDs@BN, which indicates that CDs are deposited on the surface and they are not responsible to change the layer orderliness of BN.

Formation of CDs and deposition of CDs can clearly be visualized from the TEM analysis. Fig. 1 (a, b) depicts the TEM images of CDs obtained from cigarette extract during the hydrothermal treatment. Homogeneous and monodispersed CDs are formed having an average diameter of 7 nm. It is further visualized that CDs exhibit a lattice fringe of 0.22 nm (inset image of Fig. 1 (b)) with high crystallinity equivalent to the (110) plane of graphitic carbon [37]. Deposition of CDs on the surface BN is also visualized from the TEM images of CDs@BN-1 and CDs@BN, as shown in Fig. S4 (a, b), respectively. For CDs@BN-1, the CDs are mainly deposited on the boron nitride surface. In case of CDs@BN, uniformed and monodispersed CDs are covering BN nanosheets. The degree of the formation of CDs in case of CDs@BN is higher as compared to the CDs@BN-1. More number of CDs on BN sheets may enhance the surface area, electrochemical activity and can tune the band energy of BN, which can be further analyzed by UV–vis spectroscopy analysis. From the TEM and FESEM analysis, it can be confirmed that the CDs are deposited only on BN surface and they are unable to intercalate within the BN sheets.

Optical property of CDs and CDs deposited BN was measured by UV-spectroscopy, as depicted in Fig. 1 (c). CDs solution shows a broad absorption peak around 261 nm. The absorption peak at 261 nm in case of CDs is mainly due to π-π* electronic transition [38]. Such a broad absorption can be ascribed to the random energy levels of the carbon dots and complicated band structure. UV–visible spectroscopy of CDs was also recorded at different concentrations (Fig. 1 (d)) and it is examined that absorption intensity gradually increases with increasing the concentrations (from CDs 1 to CDs 3). The dispersed aqueous solutions of CDs@BN-1 and CDs@BN also exhibits the broad absorption peaks around 267 nm, which indicates the red shifting. For CDs@BN and CDs@BN-1, absorption at higher wavelength as compared to CDs also indicates the alternation of electronic band structure, which can be further evaluated by Tauc relationship: αhν = B(hν − E
g
)
n , where hν determines the energy of photon, B signifies proportionality constant, α is the absorption coefficient, Eg denotes optical band gap, and n is number of specific electronic transition within the band due to the light absorption (here, n = 1/2) [8]. Band gap of CDs@BN-1 and CDs@BN was also calculated from the Tauc plot, as depicted in Fig. 1 (e, f). Lower band gap of CDs@BN (3.18 eV) will impact on the electrochemical activity of CDs@BN than CDs@BN-1.

Fluorescence is the inherent property for CDs. CDs solutions was excited at 370 nm and it exhibits the sharp emission peak centered at 450 nm (Fig. 2 (a)) and this is the blue region [39,40]. The CDs solution under UV light (365 nm) also shows intense blue color, as evident from the digital image, as depicted inset in Fig. 2 (a). The PL spectra of dispersed aqueous solutions of CDs@BN and CDs@BN-1 were also recorded at the same excitation (370 nm) and they also show the sharp emission peak centered around 453 nm, which implies that CDs are formed from the cigarette extract during the hydrothermal treatment and deposited on BN nanosheets. However, the intensity of the absorption peak has become diminished, which may be due to the quenching of fluorescence for CDs in presence of BN. The dispersed aqueous solution of CDs@BN also showed the brilliant blue luminescence through naked eye under UV (365 nm) light, as depicted in Fig. 2 (b).

Surface net charge is another vital parameter, which can impact on the electrical properties and colloidal stability of CDs; and surface net charge can be measured from the Zeta (ζ) potential measurement. The CDs solution exhibited negative surface charge having ζ-potential of −16.7 mV (Fig. S5 (a)), which confirms the presence of negatively charged moiety in CDs. However, CDs@BN and CDs@BN-1 accumulate more negative charge, when CDs are deposited on the surface of BN. The measured ζ-potential for CDs@BN-1 and CDs@BN are found to be −47.7 and −51.7 mV, respectively (Fig. S5 (b, c)). Aqueous dispersion of BN produces negative surface charge [7]; therefore, CDs deposited BNs show more negative ζ-potential. Due to more surface negative charge on CDs@BN, it may show better electrochemical performances as compared to CDs@BN-1.

To evaluate the electrochemical performances of CDs@BN and CDs@BN-1, comparative CV, GCD, and EIS were measured in three-electrode set-up using 1 M aqueous KCl solution maintaining the voltage range from 0 to 0.8 V. In addition, CDs@BN-2 was also synthesized using 35 ml cigarette extract solution to estimate the electrochemical efficiencies of BN in presence of more CDs content. Fig. S6 (a) illustrates the comparative CV plots of CDs@BN, CDs@BN-1, and CDs@BN-2 at 5 mV/s. From the CV plots, it is observed that CDs@BN, CDs@BN-1, and CDs@BN-2 display a quasi-rectangular type CV profile, which designates the maximum charge storage contribution through EDLC rather than pseudocapacitance. CDs and BN having high surface area are primarily responsible for the surface charge storage mechanism. However, at the particular scan rate, CDs@BN shows better capacitive behaviour than CDs@BN-1 and CDs@BN-2. The synergistic contribution from CDs and BN, homogeneous undispersed deposition of CDs on BN as well as generation of greater number of CDs are largely responsible for the better capacitive performance of CDs@BN. However, the capacitive nature has been decreased, when cigarette extract content is 35 ml for CDs@BN-2. CDs on BN is also responsible to alter the electronic band structure of CDs@BN. Better charge storage capacity of CDs@BN can further be confirmed by measuring the Sp.C from GCD analysis. Sp.C is an important parameter for supercapacitor electrode. GCD plots of CDs@BN and CDs@BN-1 at 1 A/g are portrayed in Fig. S6 (b). Sp.C of any electrode depends on the time of discharge. At 1 A/g, CDs@BN-2, CDs@BN-1, and CDs@BN display 86s, 104s, and 163s of discharge time, respectively. The Sp.C of CDs@BN, CDs@BN-1, and CDs@BN-2 was calculated using the equation S1. The obtained Sp.C for CDs@BN, CDs@BN-1, and CDs@BN-2 are 204, 130, and 107.5 F/g, respectively.

The Nyquist plot resulting from an EIS analysis can be studied for diffusion properties and electron-transfer kinetics of any electrode [41]. Real vs. imaginary part of impedance of CDs@BN, CDs@BN-1, and CDs@BN-2 is shown by the Nyquist plot. Fig. S6 (c) portrays the Nyquist plot of CDs@BN, CDs@BN-1, and CDs@BN-2. The diameter of the semicircle in the high-frequency region of the Nyquist plot can be used to determine the charge transfer resistance (Rct), and the equivalent series resistance (RESR), which is typically determined by the Ohmic resistance of the electrolyte and the interfacial resistance of the electrode, is determined by the intercept of the semicircles at the real axis [42,43]. Further, Warburg resistance (W) is designated by the straight line at the low-frequency portion [41]. Obtained Rct values for CDs@BN, CDs@BN-1, and CDs@BN-2 are measured to be 0.8, 1.2, and 1.32 Ω, correspondingly. Rct is related to the actual Faradaic electrochemical reaction, and to the electronic transfer at the electrode/electrolyte solution and the charge transfer phenomenon is very feasible in case of CDs@BN. The measured RESR values for CDs@BN, CDs@BN-1, and CDs@BN-2 are found to be 8.5, 11.6, and 13.79 Ω, correspondingly. There may be few reasons for tuning the RESR value e.g., cell design, stability of reference electrode, surface morphology and reactivity towards electrolyte, surface charge and double layer formation. The degree of deposition of CDs is higher in case of CDs@BN and it can enhance the reactivity of this electrode.

From the afore mentioned discussion, it can be concluded that CDs are formed from the waste toxic cigarette smoke through a facile hydrothermal treatment and it shows intense fluorescence in the blue region. Furthermore, morphological, UV–vis spectroscopy and PL study authenticate the deposition of CDs on the BN nanosheets. From the electrochemical analysis of CDs deposited BN samples, it can be concluded that with increasing the content of cigarette extract solution from 15 ml to 25 ml, the electrochemical performances have been improved. However, with further increasing the content of cigarette extract solution to 35 ml, the electrochemical performances of CDs@BN-2 have been diminished. Electrochemical analysis shows better electrochemical performances of CDs@BN than CDs@BN-1and CDs@BN-2. Therefore, to further enhance the electrochemical performance of CDs@BN, MoS2/CDs deposited BN (MoS2/CDs@BN) and Zn-doped MoS2/CDs deposited BN (Zn–MoS2/CDs@BN) composites were prepared using CDs@BN and different characterizations were carried out.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of carbon dot deposited BN-based composites
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 3 (a) and Fig. S7 (a) depict the FESEM images of MoS2 in both high and low magnifications, respectively. MoS2 shows flowery hierarchical architecture. During solvothermal treatment, ammonium molybdate releases ammonium and MoO4 − ions; and thiourea contributes sulphur. Sulphur ion reacts with MoO4 − to produce MoS2 and residual ammonia is intercalated in between the layered structure. Consequently, MoS2 stacking can be prevented, which leads to the growth of flower-like hierarchical architecture [44]. Hierarchical architecture of MoS2 possesses wrinkles/flakes similar to layered structure, which is responsible to facilitate the deintercalation and intercalation of electrolyte ions during electrochemical process. Similar type of surface morphology is observed for Zn–MoS2 (Fig. 3 (b) and Fig. S7 (b)). However, the nanosheets have become more wrinkled and thinner. Each flower-like hierarchical architecture consists of consistent nanopetals in the form of nanosheet. No other separated phases corresponding to ZnS or ZnO are formed during the hydrothermal treatment in presence of dopant precursor, as evident from the high magnification image of Zn–MoS2 (Fig. 3 (b)). Therefore, Zn is incorporated within the MoS2 lattice. Fig. 3 (c) shows the FESEM images of MoS2/CDs@BN. Monodispersed MoS2 hierarchical architectures are uniformly grown over the surface of BN. The degree of interaction intensity between BN and MoS2 is very enriched; and interaction as well as growth of MoS2 is more favourable on the edge of BN. For Zn–MoS2/CDs@BN (Fig. 3 (d)), same phenomenon is observed and Zn–MoS2 fully covers the BN shhets. Uniform coating and desired interaction between Zn–MoS2 and BN can enhance the electrochemical activity. However, no phase separated different morphology was observed for Zn–MoS2/CDs@BN, which implies that there are no impurities corresponding to ZnO and ZnS present in Zn–MoS2/CDs@BN.

To detect the elements, present in Zn–MoS2, EDX analysis was performed and corresponding plot is depicted in Fig. 3 (e). EDX analysis demonstrates the presence of Mo, S, O, C, and Zn and corresponding atomic % of these elements are shown in table (inset in Fig. 3 (e)). Presence of O is mostly due to the impurities on the surface and the atomic % of Zn is very less. Fig. 3 (f) displays the FESEM image of Zn–MoS2 and mapping of Mo, S, and Zn on this particular area was carried out. Fig. 3(g–i) shows the spatial distribution of these elements. Distribution density of Mo and S on that particular area is very high and covers the entire area. However, the very sparse distribution of Zn (Fig. 3 (h)) is visible, which authenticates that a small amount of Zn is doped within the MoS2 lattice. Effective Zn doping can be further established from XRD and XPS analysis. Furthermore, to distinguish the corresponding elements, present in Zn–MoS2/CDs@BN composite, EDX analysis was performed and equivalent plot is depicted in Fig. S8. EDX analysis demonstrates the presence of B, N, Mo, S, O, C, and Zn and corresponding atomic % of these elements are shown in the table (inset in Fig. S8). From the EDX analysis, it is confirmed that there are no other impurities present in Zn–MoS2/CDs@BN composite.

Elemental mapping of Zn–MoS2 was also carried out from TEM analysis (FEI technai serviced by the Center for Bionano Materials Research at Gachon University, Seongnam, Korea). Fig. S9 (a) shows the HAADF image of Zn–MoS2 and spatial distribution of Mo, S, and Zn are depicted in Fig. S9 (b-d). Intensity of distributions for Mo and S on that particular area are very high and cover the whole area. Herein also, very sparse distribution of Zn is observed (Fig. S9 (d)). Very little distribution of Zn over Mo and S confirms the effective Zn-doping within the MoS2 lattice.

To investigate the layer orderliness and crystallinity of MoS2 and other composites, XRD study was done, and corresponding XRD patterns are depicted in Fig. 4 (a). MoS2 shows two distinctive broad peaks around 2θ = 33.95° and 58.9° corresponding to (100) and (110) plane, respectively. Broadening of XRD peaks for MoS2 designates the formation of graphene like MoS2 nanosheets [45–47]. Another broad peak around 2θ = 15.3° is attributed for the (002) plane of MoS2 [45]. The XRD pattern of MoS2 is well matched with JCPDS 37–1492 indicating the layered hierarchical architecture. Layered MoS2 can facilitate the electrolyte ions diffusion. In case of Zn–MoS2, all distinctive peaks for MoS2 are present and there are no extra peaks corresponding to the indication of ZnS and ZnO formation demonstrating the incorporation of Zn within MoS2 lattice. In XRD, CDs@BN exhibits distinctive sharp peak at 2θ = 26.8° attributing to the (002) planes and this peak has been shifted to the lower 2θ (26.6°), when MoS2 flowery architecture is uniformly deposited on CDs@BN. In addition, the peaks corresponding to the MoS2 have become flatten. These observations clearly demonstrate that increased ‘d’ spacing of the BN nanosheets due to the intercalation MoS2 within the BN layers and exfoliation of MoS2. For Zn–MoS2/CDs@BN, all diffraction patterns are well defined and the peaks corresponding to the BN have been shifted to the lower 2θ. Herein also, the MoS2 peaks have also become broaden. The additional peaks present in MoS2/CDs@BN and Zn–MoS2/CDs@BN at 2θ = 41.7° and 55.26° are mainly responsible for BN. Layered structure of BN and MoS2 can influence the electrochemical behavior via facile diffusion of electrolyte ions.

To investigate the chemical configuration and chemical state of individual elements present in MoS2/CDs@BN and Zn–MoS2/CDs@BN, XPS analysis was performed. The survey plot of MoS2/CDs@BN and Zn–MoS2/CDs@BN confirms the presence of Mo, S, B, N, and C as depicted in Fig. 4 (b). The atomic % of individual elements present in MoS2/CDs@BN and Zn–MoS2/CDs@BN are provided in Table S1. However, the survey plot of Zn–MoS2/CDs@BN exhibits a peak at 1020 eV corresponding to the Zn 2p state as a result of the incorporation of Zn into the MoS2 lattice (Fig. 4 (b)). Due to the tiny amount of Zn (0.5%) present in the MoS2 of the composite material, the Zn 2p peaks present here have a very low intensity. Fig. 4 (c) displays the Mo 3d XPS spectra for these two composites and according to the figure, the peaks at 231, 225, and 228 eV are mainly responsible to the Mo+4 3d3/2, Mo+4 3d5/2, and S 2s of MoS2 [48]. These three peaks have been shifted slightly to the higher binding energy for Zn–MoS2/CDs@BN due to the doping of Zn atom into the MoS2 lattice.

Because of the minor transformation of MoS2 to MoO3 on their surfaces, the fourth peak in the composites at 235.1 eV arises [48]. The two different peaks of MoS2/CDs@BN and Zn–MoS2/CDs@BN in Fig. 4 (d) at 161.2 correspond to S 2p3/2 and 162.3 eV corresponds to S 2p1/2, revealing the existence of metal-sulphur bond and S2− in MoS2 [49,50]. Mo 3d and S 2p high-resolution XPS profiles confirm the presence of Mo4+ and S2− in MoS2. For Zn–MoS2/CDs@BN, the effective doping of Zn in MoS2 can be confirmed from the high resolution XPS spectra of Zn 2p, which exhibits two distinct peaks at 1021.6 eV for Zn 2p3/2 and 1044.7 eV for Zn 2p1/2 (Fig. 4 (e)) [48,50]. Fig. 4 (f) and Fig. 4 (g) display the deconvoluted B 1s and N 1s XPS spectra of Zn–MoS2/CDs@BN, respectively, which endorses the probable interaction between BN and MoS2 (B–S bond at 189.9 eV in B 1s) as well as BN and CDs (N–C bond at 398.7 eV in N 1s) [49,51]. Fig. 4 (h) shows the deconvoluted C 1s spectra for Zn–MoS2/CDs@BN and it shows four distinct peaks at 284.3, 285.3, 286, and 286.5 eV corresponding to CC, C–C, C–N, and C–OH bond, respectively [51]. Intense CC peak in the C 1s spectra reveals that maximum sp
2 hybridized carbon atoms are present in the CDs and this conjugated CDs can enhance the electrochemical activity. Herein also, the C–N bond formation authenticates the possible interaction between CDs and BN in CDs deposited BN. Fig. S10 (a) displays the deconvoluted B 1s spectra for MoS2/CDs@BN and there is no significant alteration of peak position of B–N and B–S bond as compared to the B 1s spectra of the Zn–MoS2/CDs@BN, which suggest that doping Zn has no influence on the chemical environment of BN during the in-situ synthesis of Zn–MoS2 in the presence of CDs@BN. Fig. S10 (b) depicts XPS spectra of C 1s for MoS2/CDs@BN and it displays four different peaks equivalent to CC, C–C, C–N, and C–OH bond same as the C 1s of Zn–MoS2/CDs@BN.

Using thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) in air flow, the thermal stability of all electrode materials was investigated within the temperature range of 35–550 °C, and the resulting plots are shown in Fig. S11. Weight loss of electrode materials around 100 °C, is due to the loss of adsorbed water. MoS2 exhibits weight loss at temperatures near 150 °C, which is due to MoS2 being oxidized into MoO3 [52,53]. However, the weight loss is a little higher than the theoretical weight loss amount, which may be owing to the release of trapped water molecules. In case of Zn–MoS2, there is no weight loss up to 200 °C, which signifies that there is a phase change within the MoS2 lattice after Zn-doping. The weight loss for Zn–MoS2 after 200 °C–350 °C is mainly due to the oxidation of MoS2 after which it becomes stable up to 550 °C. However, MoS2/CDs@BN starts to lose weight after 210 °C and after a certain weight loss, it becomes stable up to 550 °C. In case of MoS2/CDs@BN, the weight loss is more as compared to Zn–MoS2 which is mainly due to combined mass loss from the oxidation of MoS2 and the decomposition of CDs. In case of Zn–MoS2/CDs@BN, it starts to lose weight after 280 °C because of the oxidation of MoS2 as well as decomposition of CDs.

High surface area of supercapacitor electrode material is a vital parameter and high surface area can facilitate the surface charge storage mechanism. The specific surface areas of CDs@BN, Zn–MoS2, and Zn–MoS2/CDs@BN were obtained using BET surface area analysis, and corresponding BET isotherm is depicted in Fig. S12 (a). From the previous report, the surface area of pure BN was found to be 29.93 m2/g [54]. However, the surface area of CDs@BN has been significantly increased to 191 m2/g. Uniform deposition of CDs on the surface of 2D BN nanosheets as well as small sizes of CDs were responsible to enhance the surface area. The surface areas of Zn–MoS2 were measured to be 75 m2/g. In case of the composite of Zn–MoS2 and CDs@BN, the surface area has been increased as compared to their counterpart. The specific surface area of Zn–MoS2/CDs@BN was found to be 367 m2/g. Individual surface contribution of Zn–MoS2 and CDs@BN as well as the uniform growth and morphology of Zn–MoS2 on the surface of CDs@BN were also responsible for enhanced surface area of Zn–MoS2/CDs@BN. In addition, total pore volume of Zn–MoS2/CDs@BN was found to be 0.066 cc/g, which is higher than Zn–MoS2 (0.063 cc/g) and CDs@BN (0.051 cc/g). Hysteresis loops in the N2 adsorption/desorption graphs demonstrate that N2 gas condenses within the pores by capillary action, supporting the samples' mesoporous nature [55]. The pore size distribution plots of Zn–MoS2, CDs@BN, and Zn–MoS2/CDs@BN also suggest the mesoporous characteristics (Fig. S12 (b)), which is very effective for electrochemical charge storage through the diffusion of electrolyte ions. The specific surface area, total pore volume, and average pore diameter of Zn–MoS2, CDs@BN, and Zn–MoS2/CDs@BN are summarized in Table S2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical characterization of carbon dot deposited BN-BASED composites

ce:section.ce:para: Nature of electrolytes and its stable working potential can influence the activity of the electrochemical supercapacitors. Aqueous electrolytes are viable for supercapacitor owing to their low cost, eco-friendly nature, and availability. However, narrow operating potential of aqueous electrolytes restricts their use. In this work, organic electrolyte was used (1 M TEABF4/DMSO) for the evaluation of electrochemical performances of developed electrodes in three-electrode set up.

Fig. 5 (a) shows the CV profiles of MoS2, Zn–MoS2, MoS2/CDs@BN, and Zn–MoS2/CDs@BN at 3 mV/s within the voltage window of 0–1.1 V. A similar type of quasi-rectangular CV curves is observed from CV plots, which indicates that instead of the Faradic redox process, EDLC is the primary process of charge storage. However, a pair of redox peaks are obtained for Zn–MoS2 and Zn–MoS2/CDs@BN composites, which suggest the pseudocapacitive redox reaction during electrochemical process in presence of two metal centers [56]. The reversible redox peak is mainly responsible due to the reversible conversion Mo4+ to Mo6+ and vice versa. The Zn–MoS2/CDs@BN electrode has a greater area under the CV curve than other electrode materials at 3 mV/s. Sp.C, which is directly proportional to the area of the CV profile, is the most crucial metric for supercapacitor electrodes. The maximum capacitive nature of Zn–MoS2/CDs@BN is thus shown from Fig. 5 (a). CV profiles of Zn–MoS2/CDs@BN at different scan rates (2–50 mV/s) are portrayed in Fig. 5 (b). Area under the CV curves was increased with increasing the scan rates due to the fast diffusion kinetics on the electrode surface [57]. Scan rate affects current density, because the electrode flux is directly proportional to the scan rate. For Zn–MoS2/CDs@BN, the symmetric nature and better current response obtained from CV profile at high scan rates indicate quick, reversible surface reactions and ideal capacitive capabilities. The primary surface chemical reactions in Zn–MoS2/CDs@BN for the electrochemical charge storage include cation and anion transport, intercalation/de-intercalation of anion and cation, and desorption/adsorption of electrolyte ions on the surface. CV analysis of MoS2, Zn–MoS2, and MoS2/CDs@BN at different scan rates are depicted in Fig. S13. It can be confirmed that the area and current response of the CV plots have increased along with increased scan rates, based on the electrochemical measurements of all electrodes in CV analysis. The surface ion absorption caused by BN and its pseudocapacitive properties are major factors in the increased current responsiveness for MoS2/CDs@BN and Zn–MoS2/CDs@BN as compared to MoS2 and Zn–MoS2. Effective Faradic redox reaction in presence of two metal centers, the pseudocapacitive contribution dominates for Zn–MoS2. The maximum electrochemical activity of Zn–MoS2/CDs@BN may be attributed to its 3D morphology, which facilitates easy electrolyte passage through more active sites. Moreover, BN with a large surface area and conducting EDLC type CDs are responsible for providing the EDLC contribution. In the presence of CDs and BN, altered surface morphology and augmented charge storage mechanisms at the surface are responsible for improving the electrochemical efficacy of Zn–MoS2/CDs@BN. Most crucially, improved electron transportation and good capacitive performance are the result of the synergistic contribution of pseudocapacitive Zn–MoS2 and EDLC-type CDs and BN.

With similar electrode configuration and electrolyte-like CV analysis, the GCD measurement was completed to measure the Sp.C and practical applicability of all electrodes. Fig. 5 (c) shows the GCD graphs for all electrodes at 1 A/g. The electrodes' notable capacitive capability is revealed by linear charging/discharging curves. Most notably, the GCD profiles' characteristics show that they have the requisite supercapacitive nature, outstanding electrochemical performance, and appropriate reversibility. A further finding from the GCD analysis is that all materials have Coulombic efficiencies that are higher than 100%. Any electrode's Sp.C is measured by the discharge time obtained from GCD analysis. Proper materialization of pseudocapacitive and EDLC materials can have a substantial impact on the capacitive capabilities of a supercapacitor electrode. At current density 1 A/g, MoS2 exhibits a discharge time of 190 s, whereas its ternary composite (Zn–MoS2/CDs@BN) exhibits a long discharge time of 436 s, which unquestionably validates the above-mentioned fact. Sp.C of all materials were calculated using equation S1. The obtained Sp.C of MoS2, Zn–MoS2, MoS2/CDs@BN, and Zn–MoS2/CDs@BN are found to be 173 F/g, 252 F/g, 315 F/g, and 397 F/g, respectively at 1 A/g. The Sp.C of MoS2/CDs@BN and Zn–MoS2/CDs@BN have increased in comparison to MoS2, Zn–MoS2, as a result of the combined contribution via pseudocapacitance and charge storage of BN and CDs on their surface. Active sites of BN sheets and CDs are responsible to improve the accessibility of electrolyte ions absorption. Most significantly, the inorganic component BN exhibits polarity, where possible charge separation plays a crucial role in adjusting electron transport during electrochemical process. The symmetric nature of the charging-discharging profiles of all electrodes indicates substantial reversibility. The coupling of BN with CDs can produce the heterojunction, which leads to the formation of more charge carriers. BN- CDs couple shows a different type of charge transfer kinetics due to the different electronegativity of C, N, and B, which may enhance the current response without affecting the voltage window [8]. Moreover, when BN is combined with CDs, it leads to the better surface area for surface charge storage. The GCD profiles of Zn–MoS2/CDs@BN at different current densities is represented in Fig. 5 (d).

Fig. 5 (e) describes the circuit fitted Nyquist plot of synthesized materials and corresponding analog circuit is depicted in Fig. 5 (f). In the high-frequency region, diameter of the semicircle of the Nyquist plot can be considered to determine the charge transfer resistance (Rct); and equivalent series resistance (RESR), which is typically determined by the Ohmic and interfacial resistance of the electrolyte and electrode respectively, is considered by the intercept of the semicircles at the real axis [41–43]. Furthermore, the straight line at the low-frequency area signifies Warburg resistance (W). The fitted values for all electrode materials obtained from the fitted Nyquist plot are shown in Table S3. Rct of 1.23 Ω, 1.01 Ω, 1.17 Ω, and 0.85 Ω were measured for pure MoS2, Zn–MoS2, MoS2/CDs@BN, and Zn–MoS2/CDs@BN electrodes, respectively. The minimum RESR value (1.43 Ω) is found for the Zn–MoS2/CDs@BN electrode. The RESR values for MoS2, Zn–MoS2, and MoS2/CDs@BN are found to be 1.80, 1.75, and 1.70 Ω, respectively. The Rct and RESR values are meaningfully reduced for Zn–MoS2/CDs@BN. The lowest Rct value of Zn–MoS2/CDs@BN suggests preferred supercapacitance behaviour of this electrode. The obtained double-layer capacitance (Cdl) value for Zn–MoS2/CDs@BN electrode was 53.7 mF suggesting a better capacitive nature. Ideal electroactive materials for supercapacitor show the ‘N’ value of >0.45, demonstrating good capacitive behaviour. Herein, Zn–MoS2/CDs@BN shows the “N” value of 0.77 suggesting its good electrochemical capacitive characteristics.

To evaluate the individual capacitance contribution including EDLC and pseudocapacitance, theoretical calculation evaluation of the individual electrode was performed using Trasatti method. Theoretical calculation was performed depending on the CV analysis of individual electrode in three-electrode configuration. The details procedure of Trasatti method is discussed in section S2. Fig. 5 (g, h) represents the plots of square root of scan rate (V 1/2) vs. reciprocal of areal capacitance (C−1) and plots of reciprocal of square root of scan rate (V −1/2) vs. areal capacitance (C) of each electrode, respectively. In contrast to CDs and BN, which are frequently EDLC materials, MoS2 is a common pseudocapacitive material. Therefore, it is expected to have major pseudocapacitive contribution of MoS2 and theoretically it is observed that MoS2 possesses predominant pseudocapacitive contribution (Fig. 5 (i)). However, pseudocapacitive contribution of Zn–MoS2 has been enhanced as compared to MoS2, which is attributed to the major redox reaction throughout the electrochemical process in presence of two-metal center. However, the EDLC increases drastically for MoS2/CDs@BN, and Zn–MoS2/CDs@BN in presence of CDs and BN. The areal capacitance of Zn–MoS2 has been decreased more as compared to MoS2 with increasing the scan rate (with decreasing V −1/2 value), as evident from Fig. 5 (h), and this is mainly due to the major pseudocapacitive contribution of Zn–MoS2. The development of an electrical double layer by ion adsorption at the electrode surface normally has faster kinetics than pseudocapacitive reactions; as a result, the more pseudocapacitance contribution is involved for Zn–MoS2, more capacitance has been decreased at ultrafast scan speeds. The theoretical results and this trend line up perfectly.

Furthermore, to understand the reaction mechanism involved in Zn–MoS2/CDs@BN, CV analysis was also carried out at different scan rates within the potential window of 0–0.5 V using 3 M aqueous KOH in same three-electrode configuration, as depicted in Fig. S14 (a). In all scan rates, Zn–MoS2/CDs@BN shows cathodic and anodic peaks. The proportion of capacitance effect can be studied based on the relationship between scan rate (V) and peak current (i) (i = aV b) [58,59]. It is a typical linear relationship for examining the ion diffusion properties and redox kinetics [60]. From the slope, b value can be found and its value lies between 0.5 and 1. When the value of b is approximately 1, surface capacitance process mainly controls the electrochemical behavior; when b is approximately 0.5, diffusion process controls the electrochemical properties [58]. As depicted in Fig. S14 (b), the b values are found to be 0.64 and 0.59, which are very close to 0.5, indicating that the electrochemical behavior of Zn–MoS2/CDs@BN electrode material is controlled by both diffusion process and surface capacitance; however, diffusion process predominates the electrochemical behaviour.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical evaluation of asymmetric supercapacitor (ASC) device in organic (1 M TEABF4/DMSO) electrolyte
ce:section.ce:para: The Zn–MoS2/CDs@BN electrode showed the best electrochemical activity in a three-electrode configuration, when compared to other electrodes. An ASC device was fabricated using this ternary composite as positive electrode and carbon black (CB) as the active material for negative electrode. Details device fabrication processes are discussed in section S3. Fig. S15 depicts the schematic representation of the ASC device. Organic electrolyte (1 M TEABF4/DMSO) was used to examine the electrochemical performance of the ASC device.

ll electrochemical analyses including the CV, GCD, EIS, and cyclic stability study of the ASC device were performed in 1 M TEABF4/DMSO. CV profiles of the ASC are displayed in Fig. 6 (a) at various potential limits (up to 1.8 V) at 50 mV/s, demonstrating that the nature of the CV curve did not change significantly despite increasing the potential limits from 0 to 1.8 V in 1 M TEABF4/DMSO. The device's maximum operating potential has been determined to be 1.8 V based on the CV analysis. In Fig. 6 (b), CV plots within a high potential limit of 1.8 V at various scan rates are displayed. Due to the quick diffusion kinetics on the electrode surface, as the scan rate increases, the area of the CV profiles and current response increase gradually, just like the three-electrode analysis. Even at low scan rate (10 mV/s), device provide sufficient current density and the characteristics of the CV profile has not significantly changed at a high scan rate (500 mV/s). It is notified from the CV analysis of the ASC device that the capacitive performance of Zn–MoS2/CDs@BN as a positive electrode has enormous promise for practical application. The nature of the obtained CV profiles has been found to significantly depart from their ideal rectangular nature, demonstrating the electrode material's maximum contribution towards pseudocapacitance. A GCD test was run to determine the device's particular capacitance within 0–18 V potential window and the related GCD plots are displayed in Fig. 6 (c). Sp.C and energy density have both risen with expanding potential window. In 1 M TEABF4/DMSO electrolyte, the maximum Sp.C was found to be 266 F/g, whereas the maximum obtained energy density was 119 W h/kg at 2 A/g. Comparing the device to three-electrode systems, both the energy density and the working potential have greatly risen. Same as CV profiles, the GCD plots' nature have not changed while the current densities have changed (Fig. 6 (d)). Even at high current densities, the Zn–MoS2/CDs@BN based ASC exhibits good rate capability. Fig. 6 (e) portrays the ASC device's current density vs. Sp.C plot. At high current density (4.5 A/g), ASC exhibits a Sp.C of 188 F/g demonstrating its high rate capability. Appropriate materialization of EDLC and pseudocapacitive type materials as well as synergistic contribution of both Zn–MoS2 and CDs@BN are majorly accountable for the superior electrochemical properties and decent rate capability. Moreover, proper interaction and monodispersing growth of Zn–MoS2 over the interface of CDs@BN are also responsible for enhanced electrochemical performances. In GCD analysis, minimum IR drop demonstrates full capacitive contribution within the working potential range. Fig. 6 (f) shows the Ragone plot of the device and it is observed that the device shows the maximum power density of 4080 W/kg with maintaining the energy density of 85 W h/kg. Stability of this ASC device was also evaluated by performing consecutive charging-discharging cycles up to 10,000 times. After 10,000 cycles, device even retains ∼89% Sp.C of its initial value (Fig. 6 (g)). Originally, cyclic stability increased to 1500 cycles, and it is mostly attributable to the electrolyte's progressive diffusion into the electrode's pores, activating the electrodes. Structural collapse of Zn–MoS2 and partial separation of the electrode material from the current collector are the main causes of the lateral loss in cyclic stability. The device's CV analysis after and before cyclic stability test at 50 mV/s is shown in Fig. 6 (h). After the cyclic stability test, the area of the CV curve marginally diminished. This behavior is mostly caused by the structural breakdown after 1500 cycles, which also increases the device's resistance. However, current density of the device in the CV analysis has been enhanced after 10,000 cycles and this may be due to the time consuming effective redox reaction within the electrode system. Finally, the device underwent EIS analysis to understand the charge transfer phenomenon within the system. Corresponding fitted Nyquist plot is shown in Fig. 6 (i) and analogous circuit for the fitted Nyquist plot is depicted in Fig. S16. RESR value for this device was calculated to be 2.45 Ω. Ideal electroactive materials for supercapacitor show the ‘N’ value of >0.45, demonstrating well capacitive behaviour. However, ASC shows the “N” value of 0.705 suggesting its superior electrochemical activities.

Furthermore, volumetric energy density of the ASC device was measured using a formerly reported method [61]. The maximum volumetric energy density of ASC device was measured to be 3.42 mW h/cm3 and it appears to be good for real time application. The measurement processes of the volumetric energy density have been vividly discussed in section S4. Comparative electrochemical performances of Zn–MoS2/CDs@BN electrode (as an active cathode material) with others comparable reported materials are tabularized in Table S4. The comparison table established that Zn–MoS2/CDs@BN nanohybrid as an asymmetric supercapacitor electrode studied for the first time, and it revealed superior electrochemical activities.

Recently, the demand of wearable and flexible supercapacitor has been increased for practical utility [62,63]. For the multipurpose applications of Zn–MoS2/CDs@BN electrode, a flexible ASC was fabricated using this ternary electrode and ionic electrolyte [(NHEt3)+(HSO4)-], as discussed in section S5. The pictorial images of electrodes for flexible device are vividly shown in Fig. S17 (a, b). In viscous ionic electrolyte, several electrochemical tests of the constructed flexible supercapacitor were performed. Initially, CV test was carried out for the flexibility test maintaining the bending angles from 180ᵒ to 0ᵒ at 50 mV/s, as represented in Fig. 7 (a). The areas covered by the CV profiles as well as nature of the CV profiles remain same with changing the bending angles. Most importantly, the area and current density of the flexible device were found to be satisfactory. Additionally, even after 100 times bending of the device, integrated area and nature and of the CV curve remain unchanged (Fig. 7 (a)). GCD test was further performed at 4 A/g in different flexible modes (0ᵒ to 180ᵒ) to measure the capacitive activity of this flexible device (Fig. 7 (b)). GCD test reveals that there is no discernible difference in GCD profiles with different flexible modes. Flexible device had a significant Sp.C of 89 F/g at 4 A/g. Additionally, there is no discernible change in the EIS analysis of this flexible supercapacitor when bent at different angles (Fig. 7 (c)). The device's excellent flexibility is also indicated by the negligible change in RESR values at different flexible modes. Fig. 7(d–i) depict the pictorial images of electrodes having different mode of bending showing the flexible nature of the electrodes. Fig. 7 (j) represents the graphical representation of the flexible ASC device using Zn–MoS2/CDs@BN as an electrode material. The flexible supercapacitor was used to illuminate red LED to demonstrate the real time application. It was capable to illuminate red LED in bending mode (Fig. 7 (k, l)). Fabricated flexible ASC device and in its different flexible modes are depicted in Fig. S17 (c-f). As fabricated flexible supercapacitor device and its applications are clearly shown in a movie (supporting movie 1).

In conclusion, CDs have been synthesized successfully through a simple hydrothermal process from the water extract of cigarette smoke and synthesized CDs have been used in energy storage application. Electrochemical analysis demonstrated the improved electrochemical properties of BN in presence of CDs. In addition, Zn–MoS2 was further able to enhance the electrochemical capacitive performance of Zn–MoS2/CDs@BN due to the smart combination of EDLC-type CDs deposited BN and pseudocapacitive type Zn–MoS2. The resultant Zn–MoS2/CDs@BN composite electrode showed a Sp.C of 397 F/g in three-electrode configuration. Furthermore, ASC device assembled with Zn–MoS2/CDs@BN provided an enhanced energy density of 119 W h/kg with ∼89% retention of Sp.C after 10,000 GCD cycles. Additionally, a flexible ASC device was also made-up using Zn–MoS2/CDs@BN in [(NEt3H)+(HSO4)−] ionic electrolyte and it was able to glow a red LED in different bending modes. Uniform growth of Zn–MoS2 on the surface of CDs@BN, more active sites, high surface area, and facile ion conductivity due to the presence of CDs are mainly responsible for the better electrochemical activities of Zn–MoS2/CDs@BN. This methodology can also be scaled up to prepare cigarette smoke extract on a large scale. For this, the exhaust from smoke-rooms in various public places can be directly passed through water channels, purifying the smoke-contaminated air while also preparing the cigarette smoke aerosol extract. As a result, large-scale smoke extract preparation can be achieved spontaneously. Overall, this methodology for the fabrication of efficient energy storage devices based on the toxic waste cigarette smoke is an immediate solution to two major problems of the twentieth century: energy crisis and environment pollution.
Materials details and characterization techniques are discussed in section S1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of cigarette smoke aerosol extract
ce:section.ce:para: Initially, the water extract of the five cigarettes-smoke was prepared by passing their smoke through water, when smoke aerosol was passed, it was absorbed by the water and a brown solution was obtained. A glass apparatus, shown in Fig. S1, was introduced to prepare the cigarette-smoke extract in water. At first, this apparatus was filled with 65 ml water and one end was connected to the vacuum pump and a burning cigarette was fitted to another end of the glass pipe. Supporting movie 2 shows the methodology for cigarette extract preparation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Carbon dots synthesis from cigarette smoke extract and carbon dot deposited BN composites
ce:section.ce:para: To synthesize carbon dots (CDs) from the cigarette extract, 30 ml cigarette extract was initially treated at 200 °C for 24 h in a 50-ml teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave. After cooling to room temperature, a brown colored solution of CDs was obtained. Finally, the CDs was dialyzed using Pur-A-Lyzer Mega 1000 dialysis kit (Sigma Aldrich) in 20 ml Milli Q water for two days and stored at 4 °C in a refrigerator. This CDs solution was used for further investigations.

For the preparation of carbon dot deposited BN composite, initially, 50 mg of BN was dispersed properly in deionized (DI) water (5 mg/ml) through ultrasonication. After that, 15 ml of cigarette extract solution was added to the dispersed solution of BN and stirred for 2 h. Then the mixture was treated at 200 °C for 24 h in a 100-ml teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave. After that, it was cooled to room temperature and the obtained product was collected and dried at 60 °C in vacuum. The obtained carbon dot deposited BN composite was denoted as CDs@BN-1. Same procedure was followed to synthesize CDs@BN using 25 ml cigarette extract solution. In addition, CDs@BN-2 was also synthesized using 35 ml cigarette extract solution.
Preparation of molybdenum disulfide (MoS2) is discussed in section S1.

To synthesize MoS2/CDs@BN composite, initially, 30 mg of CDs@BN was dispersed properly in DI water (5 mg/ml) using ultrasonication. Simultaneously, 0.30 g thioacetamide and 0.15 g sodium molybdate dihydrate were dissolved in 30 ml DI water properly and it was mixed to the dispersed solution of CDs@BN. The entire mixture was stirred for 2 h and shifted to a 100-ml teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave. Then the mixture was treated at 200 °C for 24 h. Following that, it was allowed to cool to room temperature, and the black colored precipitate was washed using alcohol and DI water centrifugally. The product was dried at 60 °C in vacuum and collected.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of Zn-doped molybdenum disulfide/CDs@BN composite (Zn–MoS2/CDs@BN)
ce:section.ce:para: Preparation of Zn-doped molybdenum disulfide (Zn–MoS2) is discussed in section S1.

To synthesize Zn–MoS2/CDs@BN composite, initially, 30 mg of CDs@BN was dispersed properly in DI water (5 mg/ml) using ultrasonication. Simultaneously, 0.30 g thioacetamide and 0.15 g sodium molybdate dihydrate were dissolved in 30 ml DI water, and 0.015 g zinc acetate dihydrate was added. It was then combined to the dispersed solution of CDs@BN and allowed to stir for 2 h. The entire mixture was removed to a 100-ml teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 24 h. After allowing to cool to room temperature, the black colored precipitate was washed centrifugally using DI water and alcohol. Finally, it was dried at 60 °C in vacuum and collected.

Chandan Kumar Maity: Conceptualization, Methodology, Data curation, Investigation, Writing - original draft. Shrabani De: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing - original draft, Investigation. Asutosh Panigrahi: Methodology, Visualization. Sourav Acharya: Formal analysis, Writing - review & editing. Kartikey Verma: Writing - review & editing, Visualization. Myung Jong Kim: Supervision, Writing - review & editing, Visualization, Funding acquisition. Ganesh Chandra Nayak: Conceptualization, Visualization, Writing - review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition. C.K. Maity and S. De have equal contribution in this work.
